motivation: Debugging error flags and optimizing performance bounds.
whyExists: Arrays have a critical limitation: inserting or deleting an element in the middle requires shifting all subsequent elements (O(n)). For frequently modified collections — undo history in editors, browser back/forward history, task queues, LRU caches — linked lists provide O(1) insertion and deletion at known positions. They are also the building block for stacks, queues, and hash table chaining. Understanding linked lists develops essential pointer/reference manipulation skills used throughout software engineering.
realWorldAnalogy:
Imagine a treasure hunt. The first clue tells you where the second clue is. The second clue tells you where the third is. And so on until you find the treasure.

A linked list is like that — each item (clue) knows where the NEXT item is, but you have to follow the chain one by one to get to the middle or end. You can't jump directly to clue #5 without first finding clues 1, 2, 3, and 4.
simpleExplanation: To learn Node.js Developer Troubleshooting Part 1C from first principles, start by looking at how data resolves at runtime. Imagine your processor executing instructions sequentially. Node.js Developer Troubleshooting Part 1C defines how values bind, operations evaluate, and stack structures are clean.
visualDiagram:
```text
[User Input] ──► [Process Scope: Node.js Developer Troubleshooting Part 1C] ──► [Memory Address] ──► [Console Output]
```
syntaxExplanation: Verify that all brackets, colons, keywords, and assignments match the javascript syntax specifications. Avoid naming collisions by using scoped namespaces.

stepByStepExecution.desc: Allocate memory segment and load bindings.
